title: The Targets That Disappeared
summary: Every deployment causes a brief outage. Old instances vanish before new ones are ready, and users hit dead ends.

personality.role: platform engineer who triggered the deployment
personality.recurring_concern: dispatch delays and missed delivery windows for logistics customers

facts: 14 customers have opened support tickets in the last four minutes
deployment finished, all four new instances running
some dispatchers reporting stale route data
VP of Customer Success wants root cause by noon
three enterprise customers mentioned it in their Slack channels
the issue is in how the deployment interacted with the load balancer and health checks

hints.hint: when did the errors start relative to the deployment -- correlate the deployment timeline with the ALB error metrics
how long does it take a new instance to pass the ALB health check? multiply the health check interval by the healthy threshold
compare the deregistration delay with the time-to-healthy for new targets -- is there a gap where no healthy targets exist?
deregistration delay is 30 seconds but health checks need 150 seconds (30s interval x 5 threshold) -- old targets drain out 120 seconds before new ones are ready
increase deregistration delay to 300 seconds, reduce healthy threshold to 2, deploy during off-peak hours, reduce deployment batch size to 25%

glossary.Target group: A logical grouping of EC2 instances (or other resources) that an Application Load Balancer sends traffic to. Each target group has its own health check settings and deregistration delay.
glossary.Deregistration delay: The number of seconds the load balancer waits after removing a target before fully disconnecting it. During this window, in-flight requests can finish. The default is 300 seconds.
glossary.Health check: A periodic request the load balancer sends to each target to confirm it can handle traffic. A target must pass a set number of consecutive checks (the healthy threshold) before the load balancer considers it ready.
glossary.Healthy threshold: The number of consecutive successful health check responses a target must return before the load balancer marks it as healthy. A threshold of 5 with a 30-second interval means 150 seconds to become healthy.
glossary.Fail-open: The ALB behavior when every target in a target group is unhealthy. Instead of returning 503 to all clients, the ALB routes requests to all targets regardless of health status. This is by design but often produces 502 errors from unready backends.
glossary.HealthyHostCount: A CloudWatch metric that tracks how many targets in a target group are currently passing health checks. When this number drops to zero, the ALB enters fail-open mode.
glossary.Rolling deployment: A deployment strategy that replaces instances in batches rather than all at once. Each batch of old instances is removed before (or while) new instances launch and initialize.
glossary.Connection draining: The process of allowing in-flight requests to finish on a target that is being removed from a target group. The deregistration delay controls how long this process lasts.

narrative_arc.call: alert arrives, stakes established from story.md Opening
narrative_arc.threshold: player enters investigation, 502 errors during routine deployment, code changes confirmed harmless, problem is infrastructure interaction
narrative_arc.trials: deployment finished, all new instances running, but HealthyHostCount dropped to zero during the replacement window -- target health timeline shows a gap between old targets draining and new targets passing health checks
narrative_arc.revelation: deregistration delay is 30 seconds, health check needs 150 seconds (30s x 5 threshold) -- old targets leave 120 seconds before new targets are ready, ALB entered fail-open mode
narrative_arc.return: increase deregistration delay to 300 seconds, reduce healthy threshold to 2, cut batch size to 25%, deploy outside peak hours, post-incident review

components.name: ridgewell-prod-alb (Application Load Balancer)
components.role: Internet-facing ALB that receives all dispatcher HTTP requests and distributes them across the target group.
connections: Routes traffic to ridgewell-web-tg target group
Publishes HealthyHostCount and HTTPCode_ELB_5XX metrics to CloudWatch
components.failure_impact: When all targets are unhealthy, the ALB enters fail-open mode and routes requests to unready backends, producing 502 errors.

components.name: ridgewell-web-tg (Target Group)
components.role: Groups the four EC2 instances that serve the route optimization application. Configured with a 30-second deregistration delay and health checks at 30-second intervals with a healthy threshold of 5.
connections: Registers and deregisters instances from ridgewell-web-asg
components.failure_impact: The 30-second deregistration delay is too short. Old targets drain and leave before new targets can pass health checks, creating a capacity gap.

components.name: ridgewell-web-asg (Auto Scaling Group)
components.role: Maintains four c5.xlarge instances across two availability zones. Uses ELB health checks with a 300-second grace period.
connections: CodeDeploy triggers instance replacement in batches of 50%
components.failure_impact: The 50% batch size means two of four instances are replaced at once, leaving minimal capacity margin during the replacement window.

components.name: CloudWatch
components.role: Collects ALB metrics (HealthyHostCount, HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count) and application logs from the EC2 instances.
components.failure_impact: Without a HealthyHostCount alarm, the team had no advance warning when healthy capacity dropped to zero.

system_narration.data_flow: Dispatchers send HTTP requests to ridgewell-prod-alb. The ALB forwards them to healthy targets in ridgewell-web-tg on port 8080. Each target runs the route optimization application. During deployment, CodeDeploy deregisters old instances from the target group and the ASG launches replacements. New instances must pass 5 consecutive health checks (150 seconds) before receiving traffic.
system_narration.what_broke: The deregistration delay (30 seconds) was shorter than the health check time-to-healthy (150 seconds). When the first batch of old instances drained out and the second batch began draining, HealthyHostCount dropped to zero. The ALB entered fail-open mode and routed traffic to initializing instances that returned 502 errors.

resolution.root_cause: Rolling deployment deregistered instances with a 30-second drain delay while replacement instances required 150 seconds to pass health checks (30s interval x 5 healthy threshold), creating a window where HealthyHostCount dropped to zero and the ALB routed traffic to unhealthy targets in fail-open mode.

fix_criteria.description: Player identifies the mismatch between deregistration delay (30s) and health check time to healthy (150s)
Player identifies that HealthyHostCount dropped to zero during the deployment, triggering ALB fail-open behavior
Player proposes increasing deregistration delay to at least 300 seconds
Player proposes reducing batch size, deploying off-peak, or reducing healthy threshold

learning_objectives: Connection draining: deregistration delay must exceed the time new targets need to pass health checks during rolling deployments
ALB fail-open behavior: when all targets are unhealthy, ALB routes to all targets rather than returning 503, producing 502 errors from unready backends
Deployment safety: batch size, timing, and health check configuration interact to determine deployment risk
sop_steps: Confirm what happened by checking CloudWatch metrics during the deployment window. Look at HealthyHostCount (how many servers the load balancer considers ready) and HTTPCode_ELB_5XX_Count (how many error responses the load balancer returned) for the affected target group.

Find out how long old servers are allowed to finish their work before being fully removed. This setting is called the deregistration delay. Use describe-target-group-attributes to read it. Then calculate how long new servers need to become ready: multiply the health check interval (time between checks) by the healthy threshold (number of checks that must pass). Compare the two numbers.

Look at the target health timeline to confirm that the number of healthy servers dropped to zero. When that happens, the load balancer enters 'fail-open mode' -- it sends traffic to all servers, even ones that are not ready, which produces errors.

Increase the deregistration delay to at least 300 seconds so old servers keep handling traffic while new servers start up and pass their health checks. Command: aws elbv2 modify-target-group-attributes --attributes Key=deregistration_delay.timeout_seconds,Value=300

Reduce the healthy threshold from 5 to 2. This means a new server only needs to pass 2 consecutive health checks instead of 5, cutting the time-to-ready from 150 seconds to 60 seconds. Command: aws elbv2 modify-target-group --healthy-threshold-count 2
Reduce the deployment batch size from 50% to 25%. Instead of replacing half the servers at once (2 of 4), replace one quarter at a time, so at least 3 servers stay healthy throughout the deployment.
Add a monitoring alert (CloudWatch alarm) on HealthyHostCount. Set it to fire when the count drops below 2 and notify the on-call team immediately through an SNS topic (a notification channel).
Establish a team rule: no production deployments during the 5 AM to 11 AM peak window when dispatchers are actively using the system.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Health check passes before the application is actually ready
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: In this sim, the problem was a timing gap -- old servers left before new ones passed health checks. In this other failure, the health check URL returns 'OK' before the application has finished loading its data, connecting to the database, or warming up caches. The load balancer thinks the server is ready and sends traffic, but the server cannot handle it properly yet.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Build a readiness check that verifies all downstream systems (database connections, caches, configuration) before the health URL returns a success response. A simple check of 'is the web server running' is not enough for applications that need warm-up time.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Auto Scaling group kills new servers before they finish starting
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of a gap where no servers are ready, the Auto Scaling group -- which manages the fleet of servers -- detects that new servers are failing health checks (because they are still starting up) and terminates them. Then it launches replacements, which also get killed before they finish starting. This creates an endless loop of launch-and-terminate.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Set the health check grace period (the time the Auto Scaling group waits before checking new servers) to at least the time-to-ready plus the application startup time. Watch the scaling activity log for repeated terminations.

related_failure_modes.scenario: New servers get full traffic immediately and crash under the load
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of having zero healthy servers, this failure happens when new servers pass health checks and instantly receive the same amount of traffic as servers that have been running for hours. If the application needs time to warm up (load caches, compile code), the sudden load causes slowdowns or errors even though the server is technically 'healthy.'
related_failure_modes.prevention: Enable slow start mode on the target group. This gradually increases traffic to a new server over a set time (typically 30 to 120 seconds) instead of sending full traffic all at once.

related_failure_modes.scenario: Traffic distribution breaks when servers are in different availability zones
related_failure_modes.how_it_differs: Instead of all servers going unhealthy at once, the deployment removes servers in one data center zone while the other zone still has healthy servers. If cross-zone load balancing is turned off, the load balancer node in the empty zone has no healthy targets and enters fail-open, while the other zone gets overloaded.
related_failure_modes.prevention: Enable cross-zone load balancing so the load balancer can send traffic to healthy servers in any zone, not just the zone where the request arrived.

sop_practices: Before any rolling deployment, verify that the deregistration delay (how long old servers keep serving) is longer than the time-to-ready for new servers (health check interval multiplied by healthy threshold). If old servers leave before new ones are ready, there will be a gap with no healthy capacity.
Add a CloudWatch alarm on HealthyHostCount for every production target group. Set it to alert when the count drops below the minimum number of servers needed to handle traffic safely.

Use deployment batch sizes small enough that the remaining healthy servers can handle all the traffic while replacements start up. Replacing half the fleet at once is risky; replacing one quarter at a time is safer.
Schedule production deployments outside of peak traffic hours and document the rule in the team runbook so everyone follows it.
